with you.<|14.62|> <|15.56|> Do not bury this.<|17.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as to the harsh conclusion, the most troublesome aspect of this parable for many,<|7.46|> <|7.46|> I offer that the wicked and lazy slave does not need to be thrown into the outer darkness.<|15.60|> <|16.22|> He has already exiled himself.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.78|> As to this outer darkness, consider Satan's own words from Milton's Paradise Lost.<|8.48|> <|9.00|> The mind is its own place and in itself can make a segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.24|> We are nearing the end of our year with Matthew.<|5.68|>
<|5.68|> And this gospel feels like it has been an apt accompaniment<|10.68|>
<|10.94|> for all that 2020 has had to offer.<|14.00|>
<|14.00|> There has been more than enough weeping<|17.60|>
<|17.60|> and gnashing of teeth.<|18.90|>
<|20.00|> And there has been a fairly constant sense<|23.60|>
<|23.60|> of being in the outer darkness.<|25.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The hyperbolic nature of Matthew hasn't felt like much of an exaggeration in 2020.<|7.56|>
<|8.98|> And this morning we are presented with the parable of the talents.<|12.76|>
<|13.34|> And it seems pretty timely for stewardship season, doesn't it?<|17.10|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And many, many preachers choose to interpret this parable as an exhortation to recognize all that we have been given, multiply it, and give it back to God or the church.<|15.18|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> While that would be awesome, and St. John's would surely not turn your gifts away, I think<|6.64|>
<|6.64|> that feels like a pretty self-serving interpretation.<|10.28|>
<|11.32|> So let's do something different, because that's the beauty of parables, right?<|16.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Rather than approaching this parable from the literal viewpoint of the talents as measures of gold that equaled a huge sum of money,<|11.32|>
<|11.88|> one talent was equivalent to 15 years of wages for a day laborer.<|17.72|>
<|18.26|> I'd rather shift our focus to the incredible act of trust by the master to give so much.<|27.60|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the opening line of this story, Jesus tells that the man entrusted his property to his slaves, and he did so each according to their ability.<|12.02|>
<|12.40|> Then he went away, and we are told he came back after a long time.<|19.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What the man had given, extravagantly and abundantly, two of the recipients went out and grew to double what they received.<|10.48|>
<|11.08|> The third, out of fear, hid what he had received and it stagnated.<|16.88|>
<|17.62|> Just like earlier in Matthew, Jesus warning in the Sermon on the Mount about hiding your lamp under a bushel.<|25.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The man did not tell the slaves to multiply what he had given them, but two did so, inspired by the trust and the abundance shown them.<|11.70|>
<|12.70|> What they had been given was too good to hide.<|16.48|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So it is with God's grace, love, and word.<|5.72|>
<|6.72|> The slave with one talent or faith as small as a mustard seed could have seen love and grace flourish, grow beyond imagining, not by hiding what he had been given.<|21.86|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Instead, he buried the extravagant trust placed in him and lived in fear.<|7.32|>
<|8.36|> In the words of Bishop Frank Logue, talking about this third slave,<|13.16|>
<|13.94|> fear keeps hope locked in a room of doubt.<|19.42|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The third slave says, "Master, I knew you were a harsh man."<|7.54|>
<|7.54|> He does not say that everyone knows this to be true, but that he knew.<|13.42|>
<|13.42|> He says that the master reaps what he does not sow and gathers where he did not scatter<|20.46|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The slave misunderstands that the natural gift of being trusted with abundance is to be inspired to share abundantly.<|11.82|>
<|12.58|> This is about paying it forward, not paying it back.<|16.66|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God's grace and love are not finite unless buried and hidden.<|7.10|>
<|7.46|> When brought out into the light, grace and love multiply and spread.<|13.92|>
<|15.04|> If we see this parable as part of the larger narrative in Matthew,<|20.40|>
<|20.90|> the parables of the mustard seed and the sower and the city on a hill that cannot be hid—<|27.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We see that indeed abundance is given and meant to be displayed and shared.<|7.14|>
<|8.02|> We see it in the final words of Jesus to his disciples in this Gospel of Matthew,<|13.92|>
<|14.42|> what is known as the Great Commission.<|15.98|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus came and said to them,<|3.00|>
<|3.90|> All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.<|8.04|>
<|8.94|> Go, therefore, and make disciples of all nations,<|11.90|>
<|12.42|> baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit,<|17.02|>
<|17.02|> and teaching them to obey everything that I have commanded you.<|23.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Does that sound familiar?<|1.60|>
<|2.84|> I gave you all I have.<|5.06|>
<|5.74|> Go and share it.<|7.72|>
<|8.32|> Make more disciples.<|9.38|>
<|9.92|> Grow God's kingdom and share with them what I have shared with you.<|14.62|>
<|15.56|> Do not bury this.<|17.58|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as to the harsh conclusion, the most troublesome aspect of this parable for many,<|7.46|>
<|7.46|> I offer that the wicked and lazy slave does not need to be thrown into the outer darkness.<|15.60|>
<|16.22|> He has already exiled himself.<|19.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.78|> As to this outer darkness, consider Satan's own words from Milton's Paradise Lost.<|8.48|>
<|9.00|> The mind is its own place and in itself can make a heaven of hell, a hell of heaven.<|20.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Surely, living in fear and hiding the abundance of grace of God is a hell of our own creation.<|10.50|>
<|10.50|> God's grace and love are not finite unless buried and hidden.<|19.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When brought out into the light, grace and love can and will multiply and spread,<|7.66|>
<|8.48|> reaping where we did not specifically sow and enabling gathering where we did not even scatter seed.<|16.74|>
<|18.44|> Be well, my friends. I miss you.<|21.92|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.54|><|endoftext|>
